The passage highlights Louis XIV’s obsession with grandeur and extravagance, which forced courtiers into debt and ultimately drained the kingdom’s finances—his lavish spending was both a tool of control and a source of long-term financial ruin. While other options (e.g., wars, succession crises) were real issues, the text explicitly ties his economic strain to his encouragement of profligate spending habits among the nobility.
